items.front: When may IFR separation be discontinued between a SAR aircraft and the aircraft in distress?
items.back: When visual or radar contact has been established by the SAR aircraft AND the pilots of both aircraft concur. Both conditions (contact established AND pilot concurrence from both) must be met.

items.front: To what clearance limit should a SAR aircraft be cleared, and why?
items.back: To a fixed clearance limit, not to the distressed aircraft (which is a moving fix). Route clearances should be consistent with the distressed aircraft's route.

items.front: What are the IFR/SVFR separation minima from a ground missile emergency location?
items.back:
Lateral: 1 mile between the emergency location and the aircraft under radar control, or between the emergency location and the airspace protected for the route.
Vertical: 6,000 feet above the surface over the emergency location.
Use greater minima when suggested by the notifying official.

items.front: What advisories must be given to VFR aircraft near a ground missile emergency?
items.back: Advise all known VFR aircraft operating or that will operate in the vicinity to avoid the emergency location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically (or greater when suggested by the notifying official).

items.front: Under the STMCIC, which personnel group carries out TBM operations and TMIs? Under the OS/CIC, which personnel group carries them out?
items.back: STMCIC: personnel providing traffic management services (11-1-2a3). OS/CIC: personnel providing air traffic services (11-1-2b4). The enforcement groups are specific to each supervisor role and are not interchangeable.

items.front: When DCT instability forces the use of MIT or MINIT in place of TBFM metering, how long should those substitute measures remain in effect?
items.back: Until stability resumes. MIT or MINIT is a temporary delay-absorption measure for the specific aircraft affected by DCT instability — it is not a permanent replacement for TBFM metering. Per 11-1-3b1.

items.front: What Canadian entity formally delegates control responsibility to the FAA for Canadian airspace?
items.back: The Transport Canada Aviation Group. This formal delegation is the prerequisite for the FAA to apply its procedures in Canadian airspace under Chapter 12.

items.front: In Canadian Class A airspace, what flight rules are aircraft permitted to operate under?
items.back: Only IFR flights are permitted. Class A is controlled high level airspace from the base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including FL 600. No VFR or CVFR operations are allowed.

items.front: How does ATC's treatment of a CVFR aircraft differ from its treatment of an ordinary VFR aircraft in Canadian airspace?
items.back: ATC applies IFR procedures — including lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minima — to CVFR aircraft regardless of weather. Ordinary VFR aircraft do not receive separation services. The CVFR pilot's visual responsibility to the ground does not reduce ATC's separation obligation.

items.front: When a pilot requests "at least 1,000 feet-on-top" in Canadian airspace, what weather-related responsibility does the controller NOT have?
items.back: The controller does not independently verify weather. Weather adequacy is the pilot's responsibility — the pilot's request confirms conditions are adequate (at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with 3 miles flight visibility).

items.front: What condition triggers the Canadian radio-failure border-crossing climb rule, and when does the climb commence?
items.back: Trigger: the aircraft will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit. Climb timing: the climb to the flight-planned border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — NOT 10 minutes after the clearance limit and NOT only after entering U.S. airspace.

items.front: What specific form of authorization do Canadian regulations require before parachute jumping may be conducted?
items.back: Written authority from the Ministry of Transport. Verbal confirmations, NOTAM filings, emails from airport managers, or pilot assurances do not satisfy this requirement. The controller must not authorize a parachute jump without confirming this prior written permission exists.

items.front: What additional element must an airport controller include when approving an SVFR request in a Canadian control zone?
items.back: The period of time during which SVFR flight is permitted. This time specification is a required part of the SVFR approval — it gives ATC control over the operation's duration and enables planning around anticipated IFR traffic.

items.front: EDST vs radar display: what is the fundamental operational difference in their purpose?
items.back: EDST is a STRATEGIC PLANNING tool that predicts future trajectories and conflicts using current plan data, forecast winds, and aircraft performance. The radar display is a TACTICAL tool that shows actual current positions for real-time separation. EDST does not provide tactical radar separation.

items.front: What three independent conditions each require posting a flight progress strip in ERAM operations?
items.back: 1. Any NONRADAR flight. 2. Any flight plan NOT CONTAINED in the EAS. 3. Any strip deemed necessary for SAFE OR EFFICIENT operations. Per 13-1-5(f-h). Each condition stands alone; they are not cumulative.

items.front: Which automated notification codings are acknowledged on the ACL, and which on the DL?
items.back: ACL: IAFDOF (Inappropriate Altitude for Direction of Flight) and UTM (Unsuccessful Transmission Message). DL: EDCT (Expect Departure Clearance Time). Each must be acknowledged only AFTER the appropriate action has been completed. Per 13-1-9(b-d).

items.front: GPD: what are the three separate prohibitions in subparagraphs (b), (c), and (d), and what does each specifically forbid?
items.back: (b) Do NOT use trajectory-based POSITIONS as a substitute for radar track position. (c) Do NOT use trajectory-based ALTITUDE in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation. (d) Do NOT use the GPD for six specific RADAR FUNCTIONS: radar identification, position information, transfer of radar ID, radar separation, correlation, or point outs. Per 13-1-13(b-d).

items.front: After overriding a conflict probe alert in ATOP, what must the controller continue doing?
items.back: Continue to ENSURE that separation is MAINTAINED until the overridden conflict is RESOLVED. The override is not the end of the controller's separation obligation. Per 13-2-2(a)(3)(b).

items.front: CPDLC message set vs free text: which is preferred and why?
items.back: The CPDLC MESSAGE SET should be used to the extent possible in lieu of free text messages. The message set ensures proper 'closure' of CPDLC exchanges—each predefined message has a defined response protocol that the system tracks to completion. Free text lacks this structured closure. Per 13-2-4(a)(5) and NOTE.

items.front: ATOP automated coordination: what additional coordination framework applies beyond 13-2-6?
items.back: The requirements of Chapter 8, Offshore/Oceanic Procedures, Section 2, Coordination ALSO apply. 13-2-6 establishes that automated coordination constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors—but this is IN ADDITION TO, not replacing, the Chapter 8 coordination requirements. Per 13-2-6.

items.front: What happens to a PDC if the flight plan is revised or amended after the PDC was prepared?
items.back: PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted. The revised or amended clearance must be verbally issued to the flight crew. Per 14-1-1(d).

items.front: When no SID is filed or assigned by EAS, what are the controller's options for DCL Field 1?
items.back: The controller must either select a SID from the TAS menu OR, if no SID is to be assigned, select the NO SID option. The controller has discretion to choose either path. Per 14-1-3(a)(1)(c).

items.front: What happens to altitude restrictions when an aircraft on a climb via or descend via clearance is vectored off the SID/STAR lateral path?
items.back: A vector off the procedure cancels the altitude restrictions. Without an assigned altitude, the FMS may revert to leveling off at the altitude set by the pilot—potentially the SID/STAR top or bottom altitude. Per note to 14-2-1.

items.front: What is the sole operational purpose of the cancel-uplink function in the CPDLC ground system?
items.back: To clear open uplink indications in the FDB and ACL, allowing controllers to continue CPDLC operations with the affected aircraft. It is NOT an operational override mechanism—the voice DISREGARD phraseology with pilot UNABLE remains required for override. Per note 3 to 14-2-2(d).

items.front: List the standard JO 7340.2 meteorological abbreviations for: Moderate, Severe, Heavy, Light, Turbulence, Continuous, Occasional, Intermittent, Extreme.
items.back: Moderate = MOD, Severe = SEV, Heavy = HVY, Light = LGT, Turbulence = TURB, Continuous = CONS, Occasional = OCNL, Intermittent = INTMT, Extreme = EXTRM. Controllers must use these when composing weather-related CPDLC advisory/free text messages. Per note to 14-2-4(a).

items.front: What is the End Service (EOS) message, and when is it used in oceanic CPDLC operations?
items.back: EOS (End Service) terminates the CPDLC connection with the current data authority. The pilot sends it when the controller advises, after an automated transfer has failed and manual transfer cannot be completed. Per 14-3-3(a).
